opts: Test against realistic multi-step evaluation tasks; analyze reasoning; iterate on descriptions
Skipped because Claude figures it out
explain: Evaluation-driven; iteratively refine descriptions.

q: Canonical input_schema type for a tool definition?
opts: A JSON Schema object (typically type: object with properties and required)
explain: input_schema is JSON Schema object.

q: Why discouraged to use Bash with cat to read files when Read tool exists?
opts: Read has correct permissions, line-numbering, image/PDF support; Bash bypasses these
explain: Dedicated tools provide better experience and proper permissions.

q: Built-in tool for string replacements in an existing file?
explain: Edit performs exact string replacements; Write overwrites/creates.

q: For server tools like web_search, error code indicating query exceeded max length?
explain: web_search error codes: too_many_requests, invalid_input, max_uses_exceeded, query_too_long, unavailable.

q: MCP client capability for server-initiated structured input mid-task?
explain: Elicitation = server-initiated requests for additional info from users.

q: MCP base protocol's wire format?
opts: JSON-RPC 2.0 messages over a transport (stdio or HTTP-based)
explain: JSON-RPC 2.0 over stateful connections with capability negotiation.

q: Roles in MCP architecture?
opts: Hosts (LLM apps initiating connections), Clients (connectors in hosts), Servers (provide capabilities)
explain: Hosts initiate; Clients are connectors; Servers provide capabilities.

q: Why must MCP tool descriptions/annotations from servers be treated as untrusted by default?
opts: Tools represent arbitrary code execution; annotations could be malicious unless from a trusted server
explain: MCP spec: descriptions/annotations untrusted unless from trusted server. Hosts must obtain user consent.

q: True about cache_control on tool definitions?
opts: Optional property letting you cache tool definitions to reduce input cost
explain: cache_control optional; enables prompt caching on tools block.

q: You set tool_choice: {type: tool, name: get_weather}. Effect on model's preamble?
opts: API prefills the assistant message to force tool use; no natural-language preamble
explain: With any or tool, API prefills assistant message; no natural language preamble.

q: Allow natural language explanation while strongly suggesting a specific tool?
opts: Use tool_choice auto and instruct in user message: 'Use the get_weather tool in your response'
Use tool_choice none then add a fallback
explain: tool_choice auto + explicit user instruction allows preamble + tool use.

q: FALSE about text editor's str_replace command?
opts: It supports regex matching for old_str
Multiple matches return error asking for more context
explain: str_replace requires EXACT string matching, not regex.

q: Optional max_characters parameter on text_editor_20250728 does what?
opts: Truncates file content returned by view command to that many characters
explain: max_characters (added in 20250728) controls truncation when viewing large files.

q: Recommended way to convey small validation errors so agent can self-correct?
opts: is_error: true with structured, instructive message including field and what to provide
Stay silent and wait for retry
explain: Instructive, actionable error messages enable self-correction.

q: What is the role of `allowed_callers` on a tool definition?
opts: Restricts which caller can invoke the tool: 'direct' (model-callable) or 'code_execution_20260120' (only callable from inside a code-execution sandbox via programmatic tool calling)
explain: allowed_callers controls direct-vs-sandbox invocation. Values: 'direct' (default; model calls tool directly) or 'code_execution_20260120' (tool only callable from within the code execution sandbox).

q: Recommended Claude model class for complex tools and ambiguous queries?
opts: Claude Opus (latest 4.x); handles multiple tools and seeks clarification
explain: Use latest Opus for complex tools and ambiguous queries.

q: Recommended approach when documenting WHEN NOT to use a tool?
opts: Include explicit 'do NOT use this tool when...' guidance in description, with edge cases
explain: Include 'when not to use' as part of 3-4+ sentence description.

q: Recommended way to handle a known transient error inside an agent loop?
opts: Return is_error: true with retry guidance, OR retry programmatically with bounded backoff before final tool_result
explain: Return retryable error for Claude or programmatic retry with bounded backoff.

q: Built-in tool to delegate a self-contained sub-task with own scratch context?
explain: Agent (Task) tool dispatches a sub-agent with its own context.

q: A developer's project has CLAUDE.md at the repo root, .claude/CLAUDE.md, ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md, and a managed policy CLAUDE.md at /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md. When a session starts in the repo root, how are these files combined into context?
opts: Only the highest-priority file (managed) is loaded; the others are ignored
All four files are concatenated together so they all enter context
Only ./CLAUDE.md is loaded because the project file always wins
The user file is ignored when a project file exists
explain: All discovered CLAUDE.md files are concatenated into context rather than overriding each other. Managed, user, project (./CLAUDE.md or .claude/CLAUDE.md), and CLAUDE.local.md all load together, ordered from filesystem root down to the working directory.

q: A team wants every developer's machine to have one organization-wide CLAUDE.md that no individual user setting can suppress. On macOS, where must this file be deployed?
explain: On macOS, the managed-policy CLAUDE.md location is /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode/CLAUDE.md. Linux/WSL uses /etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md, Windows uses C:\Program Files\ClaudeCode\CLAUDE.md. Managed CLAUDE.md cannot be excluded by individual settings.

q: A CLAUDE.md file uses @docs/git-instructions.md to import another file, which itself contains @docs/sub.md, and so on. What is the maximum depth of recursive @-imports Claude Code will follow?
opts: Unlimited until a cycle is detected
explain: Imported files can recursively import other files, with a maximum depth of five hops.

q: In a large monorepo, a developer wants Claude Code to skip the CLAUDE.md files belonging to other teams in ancestor directories, while still loading their own. Which setting accomplishes this?
opts: claudeMdExcludes in settings.local.json with glob patterns
autoMemoryDirectory set to the local team
explain: claudeMdExcludes accepts an array of paths or glob patterns matched against absolute file paths and skips matching CLAUDE.md files. Managed-policy CLAUDE.md files cannot be excluded.

q: Auto memory was introduced in Claude Code v2.1.59+. Where does Claude Code store auto memory for a given project by default?
explain: Each project gets its own memory directory at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/. The path is derived from the git repository, so worktrees and subdirectories share one auto memory directory.

q: How much of the auto memory MEMORY.md file is loaded at the start of every session?
opts: The entire file regardless of size
The first 500 lines or 50KB, whichever comes first
The first 200 lines or 25KB, whichever comes first
Only the most recent entries from the last 24 hours
explain: The first 200 lines of MEMORY.md, or the first 25KB, whichever comes first, are loaded at the start of every conversation. CLAUDE.md files are loaded in full regardless of length.

q: A developer wants per-project personal preferences (their sandbox URLs, test data) to load alongside team CLAUDE.md, but never be checked into git. Which file is the right place?
explain: CLAUDE.local.md at the project root is for private per-project preferences; it loads alongside CLAUDE.md and should be added to .gitignore.

q: After running /compact, a developer notices that an instruction from a nested CLAUDE.md in a subdirectory is gone, but the project-root CLAUDE.md instructions are still in effect. Why?
opts: /compact deletes nested CLAUDE.md files from disk
Project-root CLAUDE.md is re-injected after compaction; nested CLAUDE.md files reload only when Claude next reads a file in that subdirectory
All CLAUDE.md files are stripped during compaction; only auto memory survives
Nested CLAUDE.md files require an explicit @-import to survive compaction
explain: Project-root CLAUDE.md survives compaction: after /compact, Claude re-reads it from disk and re-injects it. Nested CLAUDE.md files reload the next time Claude reads a file in that subdirectory.

q: A team wants instructions that apply only when Claude is reading TypeScript files under src/api/. Which Claude Code primitive is the best fit?
opts: A path-scoped rule in .claude/rules/ with paths frontmatter glob
explain: Rules in .claude/rules/ support YAML frontmatter with a paths field containing glob patterns. Path-scoped rules are loaded only when Claude reads files matching the pattern.

q: Which command analyzes the codebase and generates a starting CLAUDE.md with build commands, test instructions, and conventions?
explain: Run /init to generate a starting CLAUDE.md automatically. Claude analyzes the codebase and creates a file with build commands, test instructions, and project conventions.

q: Where on disk is a personal skill named 'commit' stored so it is available across all of a developer's projects?
explain: Personal skills live at ~/.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md and apply across all projects.

q: A skill called 'deploy' exists at the enterprise, personal, and project scopes with the same name. Which one wins?
explain: When skills share the same name across levels, the precedence is Enterprise > Personal > Project. Plugin skills use a plugin-name:skill-name namespace and never conflict with other levels.

q: A skill at .claude/skills/ship/SKILL.md and a custom command at .claude/commands/ship.md both exist in the same project. Which is invoked when the user types /ship?
opts: The slash command, since .claude/commands/ has higher priority
The skill, since skills take precedence over commands of the same name
Claude Code errors and refuses to start
explain: If a skill and a command share the same name, the skill takes precedence. Custom commands have effectively been merged into skills.

q: In a SKILL.md, what does the frontmatter field 'context: fork' do?
opts: Forks the current git branch before running the skill
Runs the skill in a forked subagent context, with the SKILL.md content as the subagent's prompt
Forks the conversation so the user can keep two threads
explain: context: fork runs the skill in an isolated subagent context. The skill content becomes the prompt that drives the subagent, which doesn't have access to the conversation history.

q: A skill is configured with 'context: fork' and 'agent: Explore'. What characterizes the Explore built-in subagent?
opts: Runs on Opus with full edit tool access
Runs on Haiku with read-only tools (no Write/Edit) for fast codebase search
Runs on Sonnet with browser automation tools
Runs on inherited model with all MCP servers preconnected
explain: The Explore subagent is fast and read-only, optimized for searching and analyzing codebases. It uses Haiku and is denied Write/Edit.

q: In a SKILL.md, what is the effect of setting 'disable-model-invocation: true'?
opts: Claude can still load the skill automatically; only the user is blocked from invoking it
Only the user can invoke the skill; Claude won't load it automatically. The skill's description is not added to context
The skill is disabled entirely on this machine
Removes the skill from the /skills menu but Claude can still call it programmatically
explain: disable-model-invocation: true means only you can invoke the skill via /name; Claude won't load it automatically. Use this for workflows with side effects like /commit or /deploy.

q: A team wants a skill that holds background context that Claude should know about when relevant, but should not appear in the user's / menu. Which frontmatter field accomplishes this?
explain: user-invocable: false hides the skill from the / menu. The description stays in Claude's context, and Claude can invoke the skill automatically when relevant.

q: In a SKILL.md, the user runs /migrate-component SearchBar React Vue. What does $1 expand to?
explain: $N is shorthand for $ARGUMENTS[N], using 0-based indexing. So $0 = SearchBar, $1 = React, $2 = Vue. $ARGUMENTS always expands to the full argument string.

q: A skill bundles a Python script in scripts/visualize.py that the SKILL.md asks Claude to run. The skill must work whether installed at the personal, project, or plugin level. Which substitution variable should the SKILL.md use to reference the script path?
explain: ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR} resolves to the directory containing the skill's SKILL.md file. It lets the skill reference bundled scripts regardless of installation location.

q: A SKILL.md contains the line `!\`git diff HEAD\``. When the skill runs, what happens?
opts: Claude is instructed to run git diff using the Bash tool
Claude Code executes git diff HEAD before sending content to Claude, replacing the line with the command's output
The line is included as literal text in Claude's prompt
Claude blocks the skill until the user approves the bash command
explain: The !`<command>` syntax is dynamic context injection: Claude Code runs the command before the skill content is sent to Claude, and the output replaces the placeholder.

q: A skill author wants to keep SKILL.md focused. Anthropic's guidance says to put detailed reference material into separate files in the skill directory. What is the recommended SKILL.md size limit?
explain: Anthropic recommends keeping SKILL.md under 500 lines and moving detailed reference material to separate files.

q: A custom subagent is defined at .claude/agents/triage.md with frontmatter only declaring `tools: Read, Grep, Glob`. Which model will it run on?
opts: Whatever model the main conversation is using (inherit)
explain: If model is omitted in subagent frontmatter, it defaults to inherit, which uses the same model as the main conversation.

q: What is the precedence order for subagent definitions when multiple subagents share the same name?
explain: Subagent priority (highest to lowest): Managed settings, --agents CLI flag, .claude/agents/ (project), ~/.claude/agents/ (user), plugin agents/.

q: A subagent is created via the --agents CLI flag with inline JSON. Where does its definition persist after the session ends?
opts: Nowhere; CLI-defined subagents exist only for that session
explain: CLI-defined subagents (--agents JSON) exist only for the session and are not saved to disk. To persist, add them as files in .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/.

q: A subagent's frontmatter declares `disallowedTools: Write, Edit` and omits the `tools` field. What tools can the subagent use?
opts: Every tool the main conversation has, except Write and Edit
No tools, since tools is omitted
explain: By default, subagents inherit all tools from the main conversation. disallowedTools is a denylist that removes specific tools from the inherited set.

q: A subagent specifies `memory: project` in its frontmatter. Where will it store its persistent learnings?
explain: With memory: project, the agent stores files at ./.claude/agent-memory/<agent-name>/. user uses ~/.claude/agent-memory/, local uses .claude/agent-memory-local/.

q: A plugin ships a subagent file at <plugin>/agents/security.md with `hooks`, `mcpServers`, and `permissionMode` frontmatter. Which fields does Claude Code apply when the plugin is enabled?
opts: hooks, mcpServers, and permissionMode are ignored for plugin subagents for security reasons
Only permissionMode is applied; hooks and mcpServers are ignored
Plugin subagents cannot have any frontmatter
explain: For security reasons, plugin subagents do not support the hooks, mcpServers, or permissionMode frontmatter fields. These are silently ignored.

q: A subagent has `permissionMode: acceptEdits` in its frontmatter. The parent session is running in `bypassPermissions`. What permission mode does the subagent actually use?
opts: bypassPermissions (parent's mode takes precedence and cannot be overridden)
plan (the safest mode is automatically chosen)
explain: If the parent uses bypassPermissions or acceptEdits, this takes precedence and the subagent's permissionMode cannot override it.

q: A plugin's manifest file lives at which exact path?
explain: The plugin manifest must live at <plugin>/.claude-plugin/plugin.json. Components like skills/, agents/, commands/, and hooks/ live at the plugin root.

q: A plugin called 'security-tools' ships a skill named 'audit'. What slash command invokes it?
explain: Plugin skills are namespaced as <plugin-name>:<skill-name>. So a plugin named security-tools with a skill 'audit' is invoked as /security-tools:audit.

q: In a plugin's hook script, which environment variable points to the plugin's installed directory so scripts can locate bundled resources?
explain: ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} is the plugin's installation directory. ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_DATA} is the plugin's persistent data directory.

q: A common plugin authoring mistake is putting components inside .claude-plugin/. Which of the following is the correct location?
opts: Skills go in <plugin>/skills/ (at the plugin root)
explain: Only plugin.json goes inside .claude-plugin/. All other directories must be at the plugin root level.

q: A developer wants to test a local plugin without publishing. Which CLI flag loads it just for that session?
explain: --plugin-dir loads a plugin from a directory or .zip archive for that session only. --plugin-url fetches a .zip from a URL.

q: In a CI script, a developer wants to skip auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md so the script behaves identically on every machine. Which flag should be added to `claude -p`?
explain: --bare reduces startup time by skipping auto-discovery of hooks, skills, plugins, MCP servers, auto memory, and CLAUDE.md. Recommended mode for scripted/SDK calls.

q: In bare mode, how must Anthropic authentication be supplied?
opts: From ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or an apiKeyHelper in the JSON passed to --settings
Bare mode does not require authentication
explain: Bare mode skips OAuth and keychain reads. Anthropic auth must come from ANTHROPIC_API_KEY or an apiKeyHelper. CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN is NOT read in bare mode.

q: A CI job needs Claude Code to return JSON conforming to a specific schema. Which combination of flags produces that?
opts: --output-format json plus --json-schema with the schema definition
explain: Use --output-format json with --json-schema and a JSON Schema definition. The structured output appears in the structured_output field of the response.

q: A `claude -p` call uses --output-format json. What top-level field contains the textual answer Claude produced, and what field contains a schema-validated payload?
opts: result for the answer; structured_output for schema-validated payload
explain: With --output-format json, the text response is in the result field. The schema-validated payload is in structured_output. The JSON also includes session_id, total_cost_usd, and per-model cost breakdown.

q: To stream tokens token-by-token from a `claude -p` call, which combination is required?
explain: Use --output-format stream-json with --verbose and --include-partial-messages to receive tokens as generated. Each line is a JSON event.

q: Which flag, when used with `claude -p`, limits the number of agentic turns and exits with an error if the limit is reached?
explain: --max-turns limits the number of agentic turns in print mode. Exits with an error when the limit is reached. --max-budget-usd enforces a dollar cap on API spend.

q: Which permission mode prompts the user on every tool use?
explain: default mode prompts on every tool use. acceptEdits auto-approves file edits and common filesystem commands. dontAsk auto-denies anything not in allow rules.

q: In a locked-down CI run, a team wants any tool not in their permissions.allow list (or the read-only set) to be auto-denied without prompting. Which permission mode achieves this?
explain: dontAsk denies anything not in your permissions.allow rules or the read-only command set. Useful for locked-down CI runs.

q: What is the evaluation order of permission rules in settings.json?
opts: Rules are merged and evaluated alphabetically
explain: Permission rules are evaluated in the order: deny > ask > allow. First match wins. Denials cannot be overridden by later allow rules.

q: How does the permission rule `Bash(git diff *)` differ from `Bash(git diff*)`?
opts: `Bash(git diff *)` matches commands starting with 'git diff ' (with a space); `Bash(git diff*)` would also match 'git diff-index'
Only the second form is valid syntax
The first denies git diff; the second allows it
explain: The trailing ` *` (with space) enables prefix matching for commands starting with 'git diff '. Without the space, would also match 'git diff-index'.

q: What is the precedence order of settings.json files?
explain: Settings precedence (highest to lowest): Managed settings, Command line --settings, Local project settings (.claude/settings.local.json), Shared project settings (.claude/settings.json), User settings (~/.claude/settings.json). Array settings merge across scopes.

q: What is the best primitive for instructions that must always run at a specific lifecycle event (like before every commit), regardless of what Claude decides to do?
opts: A hook configured in settings.json or hooks/hooks.json
explain: Hooks execute as shell commands at fixed lifecycle events and apply regardless of what Claude decides. CLAUDE.md is context, not enforced configuration.

q: A team adds a hook to run a linter after every Edit or Write. Which hook event matches this requirement?
explain: PostToolUse fires after a tool successfully completes. Use it for actions like running a linter after file edits.

q: A PreToolUse hook script wants to block an `rm -rf` Bash command and surface an error to Claude. What exit code should the script use?
opts: 2 (blocking error: stderr is fed to Claude)
explain: Exit code 2 is a blocking error: the action is blocked and stderr is fed to Claude. Exit code 0 is success. Exit code 1 (or any other non-zero) is non-blocking.

q: For a PreToolUse hook that wants to allow/deny via JSON instead of exit code 2, where in the JSON output is the decision placed?
explain: PreToolUse uses hookSpecificOutput with hookEventName, permissionDecision (allow/deny/ask/defer), and permissionDecisionReason.

q: What event fires when a subagent completes execution, and what is its matcher?
opts: SubagentStop, matched on the agent type name (e.g., Explore, general-purpose, custom names)
TaskCompleted, matched on the task ID
Stop, matched on the parent session
explain: SubagentStop fires when a subagent completes. The matcher field matches the agent type name. Stop in subagent frontmatter is auto-converted to SubagentStop.

q: A custom slash command at .claude/commands/deploy.md uses `$ARGUMENTS` in its body. The user types `/deploy staging us-east-1`. What does $ARGUMENTS expand to?
explain: $ARGUMENTS expands to the full argument string as typed. To access individual arguments by position, use $ARGUMENTS[N] or the shorter $N.

q: What does the `/skills` command do in an interactive session?
opts: Lists all currently loaded skills, lets the user toggle states, and saves overrides to settings
Triggers a sequential run of every available skill
Shows skill source code in the terminal
explain: /skills opens a menu of available skills. Highlight a skill, press Space to cycle through states (on, name-only, user-invocable-only, off), then Enter to save to .claude/settings.local.json under skillOverrides.

q: A team is sharing instructions across multiple AGENTS.md-using tools. Anthropic's recommended pattern is to keep one source of truth. How?
explain: Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md. To use AGENTS.md as source of truth, create a CLAUDE.md that imports it: @AGENTS.md.

q: A user wants to keep auto memory enabled but disable it for one specific project. What is the correct setting?
opts: Set autoMemoryEnabled: false in that project's .claude/settings.json
Disable auto memory in user settings
explain: autoMemoryEnabled: false in project settings disables auto memory for that project only.

q: A user wants Claude Code to load CLAUDE.md files from directories added with --add-dir. How is this enabled?
explain: By default, CLAUDE.md files from --add-dir directories are NOT loaded. Set CLAUDE_CODE_ADDITIONAL_DIRECTORIES_CLAUDE_MD=1 to load them.

q: Which built-in subagent runs in plan mode to gather codebase context before presenting a plan?
explain: The Plan subagent is used during plan mode to research the codebase before presenting a plan. It uses read-only tools.

q: True or false: A subagent can spawn other subagents (nested delegation).
opts: True; subagents recursively spawn workers as needed
False; subagents cannot spawn other subagents. For nested delegation, use chained subagents from the main conversation or skills
True, but only one level of nesting
Only general-purpose subagents can spawn others
explain: Subagents cannot spawn other subagents. Chain subagents from the main conversation instead.

q: A skill uses ultrathink in its body. What does this do?
opts: Aborts the skill if Claude is about to use a tool unnecessarily
Requests deeper reasoning when the skill runs
Forces the skill to run in a subagent
explain: Including 'ultrathink' anywhere in the skill content requests deeper reasoning when the skill runs.

q: A team needs to give Claude organization-wide behavioral guidance AND prevent specific tools from being used at all. Which combination should they use?
opts: Both rules go in managed CLAUDE.md
Behavioral guidance in managed CLAUDE.md; tool blocking in managed settings.json permissions.deny
explain: Use managed CLAUDE.md for behavioral guidance and managed settings.json (permissions.deny) for technical enforcement. Settings rules are enforced regardless of what Claude decides; CLAUDE.md is not a hard enforcement layer.

q: A custom slash command file lives in .claude/commands/. What frontmatter fields does it support?
opts: description, allowed-tools, argument-hint, model (the same fields as a SKILL.md)
No frontmatter; commands are plain markdown
explain: Files in .claude/commands/ support the same frontmatter as SKILL.md. Custom commands have effectively been merged into skills.

q: A skill's frontmatter contains `paths: ["src/api/**/*.ts"]`. When does Claude Code load this skill into context automatically?
opts: Never, unless the user invokes it manually
Only when Claude is working with files matching the glob pattern
Only on file Edit, never on Read
explain: When paths is set, Claude loads the skill automatically only when working with files matching the glob patterns. Reduces context noise.

q: A SKILL.md sets `allowed-tools: Bash(git add *) Bash(git commit *)`. What does this NOT do?
opts: It does not restrict Claude to only those tools; every tool remains callable
It does not require workspace trust
It does not work with disable-model-invocation
explain: allowed-tools grants permission for the listed tools without prompting, but does NOT restrict which tools are available; every tool remains callable.

q: What does `claude --agent code-reviewer` do?
opts: Spawns a one-time subagent and exits
Replaces the default Claude Code system prompt with the code-reviewer subagent's prompt for the entire session
Runs code review on the current file and returns results
Reviews the agent definition file for errors
explain: --agent <name> starts a session where the main thread itself takes on the subagent's system prompt, tool restrictions, and model.

q: What is the difference between `--allowedTools` and `--tools` CLI flags?
opts: --allowedTools pre-approves listed tools (skips prompts) without restricting which tools are available; --tools restricts which built-in tools Claude can use at all
--tools is for MCP only; --allowedTools is for built-ins
explain: --allowedTools auto-approves listed tools (no prompt). --tools restricts which built-in tools Claude can use.

q: A team wants to disable a specific subagent (e.g., the built-in Explore) at the project level. What's the right syntax in settings.json?
explain: Add Agent(<subagent-name>) to permissions.deny in settings.json. Works for both built-in and custom subagents.

q: What does `claude -p "..." --continue` do?
opts: Loads the most recent conversation in the current directory and runs the print-mode prompt against it
Runs the prompt twice for verification
explain: --continue (-c) loads the most recent conversation in the current directory. It works with -p so you can chain print-mode calls.

q: What is the purpose of the `--append-system-prompt` flag?
opts: Replaces the default Claude Code system prompt entirely
Appends custom text to the end of the default system prompt, preserving Claude Code's built-in capabilities
explain: --append-system-prompt appends to the default prompt. --system-prompt replaces it entirely.

q: Auto-compaction in Claude Code re-attaches the most recent invocation of each skill after summarization. What is the per-skill token budget?
opts: First 5,000 tokens; combined budget across re-attached skills is 25,000
All previously invoked skills re-attach in full
explain: After auto-compaction, the first 5,000 tokens of each re-attached skill, with a combined budget of 25,000 tokens across all re-attached skills.

q: A skill bundles a multi-line Bash command using a fenced block. Which fence syntax runs the commands as dynamic context injection?
explain: For multi-line dynamic context injection, open a fenced code block with ```! (the bang). Each line runs as a shell command before the skill content reaches Claude.

q: A team wants to disable shell-command execution in user/project skills as a security control, while still allowing bundled and managed skills to use it. Which setting?
explain: Setting disableSkillShellExecution: true in settings disables !`<command>` execution for skills/commands from user, project, plugin, or additional-directory sources.

q: A subagent file uses `isolation: worktree` in frontmatter. What happens when the subagent runs?
opts: The subagent runs in a temporary git worktree, giving it an isolated copy of the repository
The subagent runs in a sandboxed Docker container
The subagent inherits the parent worktree but cannot modify files
explain: isolation: worktree runs the subagent in a temporary git worktree. The worktree is automatically cleaned up if the subagent makes no changes.

q: A team is debating subagents vs. agent teams for a parallel research workflow. Which is correct?
opts: They are the same feature with different names
Subagents work within a single session; agent teams coordinate across separate sessions and let workers communicate with each other
Agent teams replace subagents in v2.1.59+
Subagents cost more credits than agent teams
explain: Subagents work within a single session. Agent teams coordinate across separate sessions and let teammates communicate.

q: Which built-in subagent is used when Claude needs to handle complex tasks requiring both exploration AND modification?
explain: general-purpose has all tools and inherits the main conversation's model. Use it for tasks needing exploration plus modification.

q: A developer creates a hook script that processes `tool_input.command` from stdin. What format is the hook input?
explain: Hook scripts receive event JSON on stdin. Common fields: session_id, transcript_path, cwd, permission_mode, hook_event_name, tool_name, tool_input, tool_use_id.

q: A hook is configured at .claude/settings.json with `"PreToolUse": [{"matcher": "Bash"}, ...]`. The matcher 'Bash' is treated as which kind of pattern?
opts: A regex that matches partial tool names
explain: Matchers containing only letters, digits, underscores, and pipes are treated as exact match or list match. Other characters trigger regex mode.

q: A team wants a hook that fires only for MCP tools from the 'memory' server. Which matcher pattern should they use?
explain: MCP tools follow the pattern mcp__<server>__<tool>. Use a regex matcher like mcp__memory__.* to match all tools from a specific server.

q: What hook event lets a script modify the user's prompt or block it before Claude processes it?
explain: UserPromptSubmit fires when the user submits a prompt, before Claude processes it. It can block (decision: 'block') and inject additionalContext.

q: In a hook config, what does the Stop hook returning exit code 2 do?
opts: Forces a hard exit of Claude Code
Prevents Claude from stopping (continues the conversation)
Crashes the session with an error
explain: For Stop, SubagentStop, and TeammateIdle events, exit code 2 prevents stopping. For PreToolUse it blocks the tool call.

q: In a SessionStart hook, how can a script set environment variables that persist for the rest of the session?
opts: Write export statements to the path in the CLAUDE_ENV_FILE environment variable
Modify settings.json's env field at runtime
explain: SessionStart, Setup, CwdChanged, and FileChanged hooks have access to CLAUDE_ENV_FILE. Writing `export VAR=value` lines to that file persists them.

q: A `claude -p` invocation includes `--permission-mode acceptEdits`. Beyond auto-approving file edits, what else is auto-approved?
opts: Common filesystem commands such as mkdir, touch, mv, and cp
explain: acceptEdits lets Claude write files without prompting and auto-approves common filesystem commands (mkdir, touch, mv, cp). Other shell commands still need allow rules.

q: A subagent's `background: true` frontmatter does what?
opts: Runs the subagent in a Docker container
Always runs the subagent as a background task, concurrent with the main conversation
Logs subagent activity to a background file
explain: background: true sets the subagent to always run in the background, concurrent with the main conversation. Permissions are prompted upfront.

q: A team is choosing between skills and CLAUDE.md for a multi-step deployment procedure. Anthropic's guidance suggests:
opts: Move multi-step procedures from CLAUDE.md to a skill, since skill bodies load on demand and don't consume context until used
explain: Anthropic guidance: keep CLAUDE.md to facts Claude should hold every session. Move multi-step procedures to a skill or path-scoped rule.

q: What does the InstructionsLoaded hook event provide?
opts: A list of all CLAUDE.md, .claude/rules/*.md, and similar files that get loaded, with file_path, memory_type, load_reason, globs
A summary of the previous session
explain: InstructionsLoaded fires when CLAUDE.md or rules files load. Inputs include file_path, memory_type, load_reason (session_start, nested_traversal, path_glob_match, include, compact).

q: A team needs all `.env` reads to be denied. The recommended pattern is:
explain: For technical enforcement of file access blocks, use permissions.deny in settings.json. CLAUDE.md is not a hard enforcement layer for security.

q: To make a CLAUDE.md note visible only to human maintainers (not Claude), what syntax should be used?
opts: Block-level HTML comments: <!-- maintainer notes --> are stripped before content reaches Claude
explain: Block-level HTML comments in CLAUDE.md are stripped before injection into Claude's context. Use them for human-only notes.

q: What is the recommended size for an effective CLAUDE.md file?
opts: Under 200 lines per CLAUDE.md file; longer files reduce adherence
explain: Anthropic recommends targeting under 200 lines per CLAUDE.md file. Longer files consume more context and reduce adherence.

q: A developer wants to verify which CLAUDE.md files are loaded for the current session. Which command shows this?
explain: /memory lists all CLAUDE.md, CLAUDE.local.md, and rules files loaded, lets you toggle auto memory, and provides a link to open the auto memory folder.

q: What is the difference between `/clear` and `/compact`?
opts: /clear empties context entirely; /compact summarizes history to free tokens, then re-injects project-root CLAUDE.md and recent skill invocations
/compact requires a paid plan; /clear is free
explain: /clear empties the conversation context entirely. /compact summarizes history and continues the session, re-injecting project-root CLAUDE.md.

q: True or false: A subagent inherits skills from the parent conversation by default.
opts: False; subagents do not inherit skills from the parent. Use the `skills` field to inject skill content into the subagent's context at startup
explain: Subagents do NOT inherit skills from the parent. Use the skills frontmatter field to preload specific skills into the subagent's context.

q: In a SKILL.md, the field `argument-hint: "[issue-number]"` does what?
opts: Validates that an issue number is passed
Provides autocomplete hint shown to the user when typing /skill-name
explain: argument-hint shows a hint during autocomplete. It does NOT validate input. UI affordance only.

q: How can a skill be instructed to do its work in an isolated subagent context, while the user invokes it from the main conversation?
opts: Set `context: fork` and optionally `agent: <type>` in the skill frontmatter
explain: context: fork runs the skill in a forked subagent context. agent selects the subagent type (Explore, Plan, general-purpose, custom).

q: A skill has `name: my_skill_name` (with underscores). Will it load?
opts: No; name must be lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only (max 64 chars)
explain: The name field must be lowercase letters, numbers, and hyphens only (max 64 characters). Underscores are NOT allowed.

q: What does setting `agent` in plugin's settings.json do?
opts: Activates one of the plugin's custom agents as the main thread when the plugin is enabled
explain: A plugin's settings.json with `agent: "<name>"` activates one of the plugin's custom agents as the main thread. Only `agent` and `subagentStatusLine` keys are supported in plugin settings.json.

q: A team is choosing between hooks and CLAUDE.md for an instruction like "always run gofmt before committing." Which is more reliable?
opts: A hook that runs gofmt on PostToolUse for Edit/Write or PreToolUse for git commit, since hooks execute deterministically
explain: For instructions that must run at a specific lifecycle event regardless of what Claude decides, use a hook. CLAUDE.md is context, not enforced configuration.

q: A team uses `--agents` JSON to define multiple subagents at startup. Which fields can the JSON contain?
opts: The same frontmatter fields as file-based subagents
explain: The --agents flag accepts JSON with the same frontmatter fields as file-based subagents (description, prompt, tools, disallowedTools, model, etc.).

q: A developer is debugging why a managed CLAUDE.md isn't applied. Which command lists the active managed sources?
explain: /status shows which settings sources are active and their origin. /memory lists CLAUDE.md and rule files.

q: A team wants `claude --agent reviewer` to be the default for every session in a project. Where does this go?
explain: Set `agent: "reviewer"` in .claude/settings.json. The CLI flag --agent overrides the setting if both are present.

q: Plugin version management: a plugin omits the `version` field in plugin.json. What is used as the version?
opts: If distributed via git, the commit SHA is used and every commit counts as a new version
explain: If version is omitted and the plugin is distributed via git, the commit SHA is used. If version is set, users only receive updates when bumped.

q: When multiple PreToolUse hooks each return different decisions, which decision wins?
opts: deny > defer > ask > allow (most restrictive wins)
explain: Precedence is deny > defer > ask > allow. The most restrictive decision wins; a single deny cannot be overridden.

q: True or false: settings.local.json files cannot redirect autoMemoryDirectory to a custom location.
opts: True; autoMemoryDirectory is only accepted from policy and user settings, and from --settings
False; any settings file can set it
False; only the user can set it
explain: autoMemoryDirectory must be absolute or ~/-prefixed. Accepted ONLY from policy and user settings, and from --settings. NOT from project or local settings (security: cloned repos could redirect writes).

q: A team runs Claude Code in plan mode for changes under src/billing/. Which CLAUDE.md pattern accomplishes this?
opts: Add behavioral guidance to CLAUDE.md (it's behavioral guidance, not enforced)
explain: For behavioral guidance like 'use plan mode for X', use CLAUDE.md or path-scoped rules. For enforcement, use permissions rules.

q: What is the difference between standalone configuration in `.claude/` and a packaged plugin?
opts: Standalone (.claude/) gives short skill names like /hello and is best for personal use; plugins use namespaced names like /plugin:hello and are best for sharing
explain: Standalone uses short names. Plugins use plugin-name:skill-name namespacing to prevent conflicts and are best for sharing/versioning.

q: A subagent file has `color: purple` in its frontmatter. What does this do?
opts: Sets the display color shown in the task list and transcript
explain: color sets a display color for the subagent. Values: red, blue, green, yellow, purple, orange, pink, cyan.

q: A subagent declares `maxTurns: 10`. What happens when the limit is hit?
opts: The subagent stops after 10 agentic turns
Claude switches to a different model
explain: maxTurns sets the maximum number of agentic turns before the subagent stops. Protects against runaway agents and bounds cost.

q: A skill body's content is loaded into the conversation as a single message and stays for the rest of the session. Why does this matter?
opts: Because it triples token usage per turn
Because every line is a recurring token cost across all subsequent turns; concise skill bodies are essential
Because the skill cannot be re-invoked
explain: Skill content stays in context across all subsequent turns. Every line is a recurring token cost. State what to do, not how/why.

q: In a CI pipeline, prior code-review findings need to be deduplicated against new ones. The official approach is to:
opts: Tell Claude to check Git history
Pass prior findings as context (e.g., via system prompt append or stdin) and instruct Claude to deduplicate before reporting
explain: Pass prior findings as context (e.g., via --append-system-prompt-file or stdin) and instruct Claude to deduplicate. Use --output-format json for machine parsing.

q: A team has skills with overlapping descriptions. Anthropic's guidance to make a skill trigger more reliably is to:
opts: Strengthen the description with specific keywords users would naturally say, OR use disable-model-invocation if only manual invocation is desired
Move the skill to enterprise scope
explain: For triggering issues: check description includes natural keywords; rephrase the request; or use disable-model-invocation: true.

q: A team is migrating prompts from Claude Opus 4.6 to Claude Opus 4.7 for an extraction pipeline. They observe that an instruction "format all sections with bullet points" only formats the first section. What is the most likely cause?
opts: Opus 4.7 has a token limit on bullet outputs that truncates later sections
Opus 4.7 interprets prompts more literally and will not silently generalize an instruction across items unless scope is stated explicitly
Opus 4.7 requires markdown to be enabled via output_config to render bullets
Opus 4.7 disables list formatting by default at low effort levels
explain: Opus 4.7 interprets prompts more literally than 4.6. State scope explicitly: 'Apply this formatting to every section, not just the first one.'

q: Code review on Opus 4.6 reported 100 issues per PR. After upgrading to Opus 4.7 with the same prompt that says 'only report high-severity issues, be conservative,' recall on a benchmark drops noticeably. Recommended fix?
opts: Lower effort to medium so the model investigates more findings
Switch the prompt to coverage mode: report every issue including low-confidence ones, with confidence + severity for downstream filtering
Disable adaptive thinking and use manual extended_thinking with budget_tokens
explain: Opus 4.7 follows 'be conservative' faithfully, suppressing low-severity findings. Move filtering downstream: report everything, then filter.

q: How many examples should a multishot triage prompt include and how should they be structured?
opts: 1 example wrapped in a markdown code block
3-5 examples wrapped in <example> tags inside a parent <examples> tag
Exactly 7 examples in JSON array format inside the system prompt
explain: Anthropic recommends 3-5 examples for best results, wrapped in <example> tags inside <examples>. Should be relevant, diverse, and structured.

q: For a 50-document research task on Opus 4.7, where should the documents and the user query be placed in the prompt for best performance?
opts: Documents at the bottom, query at the top
Documents at the top, query at the end (~30% improvement on multi-document inputs)
Documents in the system prompt and query in the assistant turn
explain: Place longform data near the top of your prompt; queries at the end can improve quality up to 30% on multi-document inputs.

q: When wrapping multiple documents for Claude, which XML structure does Anthropic's prompting guide recommend?
explain: Wrap each document in <document> tags inside a <documents> parent, with <document index="n"> attributes and nested <source> + <document_content>.

q: On Claude Opus 4.7, what happens if you send a request with thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": 10000}?
opts: The request succeeds and Claude uses 10000 thinking tokens
The request succeeds but budget_tokens is ignored
The API returns a 400 error because manual extended thinking is no longer supported on Opus 4.7
Claude silently falls back to adaptive thinking
explain: Manual extended thinking is no longer supported on Opus 4.7+. Use adaptive thinking instead.

q: Migration from extended_thinking with budget_tokens to adaptive thinking on Opus 4.7?
explain: Adaptive thinking: thinking={"type":"adaptive"} + output_config.effort. Budget control via the effort parameter.

q: For coding and agentic use cases on Claude Opus 4.7, recommended effort default?
explain: Anthropic recommends starting with the new 'xhigh' effort level for coding and agentic use cases on Opus 4.7.

q: You enable Structured Outputs with output_config.format and also set citations.enabled=true on a document block. What happens?
opts: Request succeeds with citations in JSON
API returns 400; Citations and Structured Outputs are incompatible
explain: Citations cannot be used together with Structured Outputs. Returns 400.

q: Maximum tools that can have strict: true in a single request?
explain: Structured Outputs limits 20 tools with strict: true per request.

q: Which Python SDK helper is used for Structured Outputs with Pydantic?
explain: client.messages.parse() with output_format=PydanticModel.

q: In the TypeScript SDK, which helper converts a Zod schema for Structured Outputs?
explain: zodOutputFormat from @anthropic-ai/sdk/helpers/zod, used inside output_config.format.

q: Citations rule for enabling across multiple documents?
opts: All or none within a request
explain: Citations must be enabled on all or none of the documents within a request.

q: Citation location format for plain text documents?
explain: Plain text uses 0-indexed character indices with exclusive end indices.

q: Citation location format for PDF documents?
explain: PDF citations use start_page_number and end_page_number as 1-indexed page numbers with exclusive end.

q: How are output tokens charged for the cited_text field?
opts: Provided for convenience and does NOT count toward output tokens
Charged at 50% of standard output rate
explain: cited_text is provided for convenience and does not count toward output tokens. When passed back, also doesn't count toward input.

q: Discount on Batch API requests vs standard Messages API?
explain: Batch API charges 50% of standard prices for both input and output tokens.

q: Maximum requests in a single Message Batch?
explain: Limited to 100,000 Message requests OR 256 MB, whichever is reached first.

q: Maximum batch payload size?
explain: 100,000 requests OR 256 MB in size, whichever first.

q: How long does the Message Batches API have to complete before requests expire?
explain: Batches expire if processing does not complete within 24 hours.

q: How long are batch results available for download after batch creation?
explain: Batch results are available for 29 days after creation.

q: Field correlating each request inside a batch to its result?
explain: Each request must include a unique custom_id matching ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]{1,64}$.

q: Is the Message Batches API eligible for Zero Data Retention (ZDR)?
explain: Batch processing is NOT ZDR-eligible.

q: Should mandatory pre-merge review (within 2 minutes) use Batch API or standard Messages API?
opts: Standard Messages API; Batch can take up to 24 hours
explain: Batch is async; pre-merge gates need synchronous Messages API.

q: What IS supported in a single batched request?
opts: Tool use, vision, multi-turn, system messages, beta features
explain: Any request you can make to standard Messages API can be batched.

q: On Mythos Preview, what happens with prefilled assistant messages?
explain: On Claude 4.6+ and Mythos Preview, prefilled responses on the last assistant turn return 400.

q: Migration from prefill to force JSON output on Claude 4.6+?
explain: Migrate to Structured Outputs (output_config.format) for schema-conformance.

q: Which tool_choice values are compatible with extended/adaptive thinking?
explain: With thinking enabled, only auto (default) and none are supported. any and tool return errors.

q: To force the model to call exactly one of your tools (with strict: true)?
explain: tool_choice: {"type":"any"} forces a tool call. Incompatible with thinking enabled.

q: Strict tool use guarantees what about tool_use blocks?
opts: Syntactic schema conformance only, NOT semantic correctness
explain: Strict tool use eliminates schema validation errors but doesn't guarantee semantic correctness.

q: Why is calculated_total + stated_total schema redundancy useful?
opts: Mismatch flags arithmetic/OCR errors for human review
explain: Schema redundancy lets you flag mismatches automatically.

q: Prevent fabrication on optional fields during extraction?
opts: Use optional/nullable fields so the model can return null
Use enum constraints on every string
explain: Optional/nullable fields prevent fabrication when info is missing from source.

q: Resilient design pattern for enum classification?
opts: enum + 'other' value combined with free-text detail field
explain: enum + 'other' + detail string lets Claude classify cleanly when fits and gracefully escape when doesn't.

q: Validation-retry loop fixes which class of error and fails on which?
opts: Fixes format/syntax errors but NOT missing information
Fixes missing info by inventing values
explain: Retry loops fix format errors but cannot fix info missing from source (just produces confident hallucinations).

q: Why use INDEPENDENT reviewer instead of self-review?
opts: Independent reviewers don't share generator's mental model, catching errors self-review rationalizes away
explain: Self-review tends to confirm prior choices. Independent reviewer is fresher.

q: Multi-pass review architecture for 100-file PR?
opts: Single review with all 100 files concatenated
Per-file local review (deep) + cross-file integration review
explain: Multi-pass: per-file local + cross-file integration. Different context, different bug classes.

q: Track and suppress recurring false-positive patterns?
explain: detected_pattern labels findings with recognizable names; build a suppression list of false-positive patterns.

q: Maximum output token cap on Claude Opus 4.7 and Opus 4.6?
explain: Opus 4.7 and 4.6 support up to 128k output tokens.

q: Maximum output token cap on Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Haiku 4.5?
explain: Sonnet 4.6 and Haiku 4.5 cap at 64k output tokens.

q: Beta header for Batch API requests up to 300k output tokens?
explain: output-300k-2026-03-24 enables 300k output tokens in batch.

q: Model identifier for Claude Opus 4.7?
explain: Exact ID: claude-opus-4-7.

q: Long-document grounding technique?
opts: Extract relevant quotes into <quotes> tags before answering
explain: Ask Claude to find quotes first inside <quotes> tags, then answer. Grounds responses, reduces hallucination.

q: Tags for separating reasoning from final output (manual CoT)?
explain: <thinking> and <answer> tags cleanly separate reasoning from output.

q: In multishot, what does Anthropic recommend showing in ambiguous cases?
opts: Show ambiguous-case reasoning explicitly inside <thinking> tags within examples
explain: Use <thinking> tags inside examples to show reasoning patterns for edge cases.

q: Setting role 'You are a senior tax attorney' in the system prompt is what technique?
explain: Role assignment focuses Claude's behavior, vocabulary, and tone.

q: Positive examples vs negative examples?
opts: Positive examples showing how Claude SHOULD
explain: Tell Claude what to do instead of what not to do.

q: cited_text behavior when passed back in subsequent turns?
opts: Counts as input at full rate
Does NOT count toward input tokens
explain: When passed back, cited_text doesn't count toward input tokens.

q: How long are compiled grammars cached for Structured Outputs?
explain: Compiled grammars cached up to 24 hours, invalidated by schema structure or tool-set changes.

q: Combining prompt caching with citations?
opts: Compatible: docs cached via cache_control, citation blocks themselves can't be cached
explain: Citations works with prompt caching. Source docs can be cached via cache_control.

q: For batches that take longer than 5 minutes, prompt-caching configuration?
explain: Use 1-hour cache duration since batches can run >5 minutes.

q: Format constraint for custom_id on a batched request?
explain: 1-64 chars, alphanumeric/hyphen/underscore.

q: JSON Schema feature NOT supported by Structured Outputs?
explain: Numerical constraints (minimum, maximum, multipleOf) NOT supported. Also: string length, recursive schemas, complex enums.

q: Sonnet 4.6 default effort if not explicitly set?
explain: Sonnet 4.6 defaults to high effort, unlike Sonnet 4.5.

q: After tool_result, when continuing with thinking enabled, how to handle prior thinking blocks?
opts: Pass them back UNMODIFIED in the assistant turn alongside tool_use
explain: Pass thinking blocks back unmodified. The signature ensures consistent reasoning.

q: Can you toggle thinking mid-turn during a tool-use loop on Sonnet 4.6?
opts: No: tool-use loops are one assistant turn; only toggle at NEW user turns
explain: Tool-use loops are part of single assistant turn. Mid-turn toggle gracefully degrades by disabling thinking for that request.

q: Interleaved thinking on Sonnet 4.6 (manual) requires which beta header?
explain: interleaved-thinking-2025-05-14 for manual. Adaptive thinking needs no header. Mythos/4.7 automatic.

q: For HIPAA workloads using Structured Outputs, documented warning?
opts: Do NOT include PHI in schema definitions; PHI only in message content
explain: Schemas cached separately from prompts. PHI must NOT be in schema definitions; only in message content.

q: Stop_reason indicating Claude refused for safety?
explain: stop_reason: 'refusal' indicates safety refusal. Output may not match schema; tokens still charged.

q: Stop_reason 'max_tokens' with Structured Outputs behavior?
opts: Output may be incomplete and may not match schema; retry with higher max_tokens
explain: On max_tokens, output incomplete and may not match schema. Retry with higher max_tokens.

q: Anthropic's 'Golden rule' of prompting?
opts: Show prompt to colleague with minimal context; if confused, Claude will be too
explain: If a colleague would be confused by your prompt, Claude will too.

q: For complex research with hypothesis tracking, recommended prompt?
opts: Develop competing hypotheses, track confidence in progress notes, self-critique, persist hypothesis tree
explain: Prompt Claude to develop competing hypotheses, track confidence, self-critique, persist research notes.

q: For autonomous coding agents on Sonnet 4.6, effort matching?
opts: Multi-step agents/computer use: high; bimodal: adaptive; latency-sensitive: medium
explain: Autonomous multi-step: high. Bimodal mixes: adaptive. Latency-sensitive coding: medium.

q: Token billing for thinking on Claude 4.6+?
opts: Full thinking tokens billed; display: omitted saves latency NOT cost
explain: Full thinking tokens always billed. Display setting affects latency, not cost.

q: Recommended chunking for plain text in Citations?
opts: Sentence chunking; can cite single sentence or chain consecutive
explain: Sentence chunking. Claude can cite a single sentence or chain consecutive sentences.

q: Structured Outputs + batch processing?
explain: Structured Outputs works with Batch API and inherits the 50% discount.

q: What does 'validation-retry loop fails for missing-info errors' mean?
opts: Re-prompting can't recover info not in source; produces confident hallucinations; fall back to error or human review
explain: Retries fix format errors but produce hallucinations on missing-info errors. Surface error and route to human review.

q: Recommended thinking strategy for bimodal workloads (mix of easy and hard)?
opts: Adaptive thinking; skips on simple, deepens on complex; evaluate medium and high
explain: Adaptive is well-suited to bimodal: skips simple, reasons deeply on complex. Evaluate medium and high effort.

q: Opus 4.7's tendency to use FEWER subagents than 4.6 is described as?
opts: Generally producing better results; raise effort to high or xhigh for more tool use
explain: 4.7 uses tools/subagents less by default, which produces better results. Raise effort to encourage more.

q: Adaptive thinking depth determined by which two factors?
opts: Model name and time of day
explain: Adaptive thinking calibrates based on effort parameter and query complexity.

q: Document blocks NOT supported for citations?
opts: csv, xlsx, docx, md, txt files
explain: csv, xlsx, docx, md, txt not supported as document blocks. Convert to plain text. Supported: plain text, PDF, custom content.

q: In streaming, which delta type carries a single citation entry?
explain: content_block_delta with delta.type 'citations_delta' contains a single citation appended.

q: Feature enabling Claude on 4.6/4.5 to track remaining context window?
opts: Context awareness in Sonnet 4.6 / Sonnet 4.5 / Haiku 4.5
explain: 4.6 and 4.5 models track their remaining context window throughout a conversation.

q: For multi-context-window agentic workflows, often preferred over compaction?
opts: Starting fresh with new context window; have model rediscover state from local filesystem
explain: 4.6 models effective at discovering state from filesystem. Fresh window with state rediscovery often beats compaction.

q: Prevent Claude from removing tests for convenience in long agentic runs?
opts: 'You may delete failing tests if needed'
'Unacceptable to remove or edit tests; could lead to missing or buggy functionality'
explain: Explicitly state tests must not be removed or edited.

q: Recommended fallback when Opus 4.6 still over-explores after 'don't be overly thorough' prompting?
explain: Hierarchy: targeted prompt -> remove over-prompting -> lower effort.

q: On Opus 4.7, prose style for long-form is more direct/opinionated. For warmer tone?
opts: Add explicit style instruction: 'Use a warm, collaborative tone. Acknowledge user's framing'
explain: Re-evaluate style prompts; add explicit warm-tone instruction.

q: Combining batch processing with prompt caching on shared context?
opts: Batch (50% off) + 1-hour prompt cache breakpoint on shared context
explain: Batch (50% off) + 1-hour cache duration produces compounding cost reductions and high cache-hit rates.

q: On adaptive thinking, recommended steering for OVER-thinking?
opts: 'Thinking adds latency... only when it improves answer quality... when in doubt, respond directly'
explain: Adaptive thinking triggering is promptable. Steer with explicit guidance and use effort as fallback.

q: Most common explicit prompt-chaining pattern?
explain: Self-correction chaining: each step a separate API call for inspection/branching.

q: Key change in Claude 4.7 vs 4.6 default behavior?
opts: Spawns FEWER subagents by default; uses tools less; calibrates verbosity to task; provides regular progress updates
explain: 4.7 spawns fewer subagents, uses tools less, more regular progress. Steerable via prompting/effort.

q: A research orchestrator running on Claude Opus 4.7 needs to process a 750k-token corpus in a single request. Which model and configuration is correct as of the 1M context GA in 2026?
opts: Claude Opus 4.7 with no beta header (1M context is GA)
Claude Opus 4.7 with the context-1m-2025-08-07 beta header
Claude Haiku 4.5 with a premium pricing flag for >200k
Claude Sonnet 4.5 with the long-context-2026 beta header
explain: As of 2026 GA, Claude Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6 support a 1M-token context window without any beta header and without premium pricing. Haiku 4.5 remains 200k. No long-context beta is required.

q: A support agent on Claude Haiku 4.5 receives a 220k-token conversation history. What is the correct expectation?
opts: Haiku 4.5 supports 1M context like the Opus and Sonnet 4.6 family
The request will exceed Haiku 4.5's 200k context window
Haiku 4.5 will silently truncate to fit
explain: Haiku 4.5 has a 200k-token context window. Only Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos Preview have 1M. Newer Claude models return a validation error rather than silently truncating when prompt + output tokens exceed the window.

q: You place a critical 'Extraction Rules' section in the middle of a 400k-token prompt. Why does Claude miss several rules?
opts: The model only reads the first and last 100k tokens
Lost-in-the-middle: recall accuracy degrades for content placed in the middle of long contexts
Claude strips middle blocks for caching reasons
Middle blocks are processed by a smaller distilled model
explain: Anthropic's effective context engineering guidance describes context rot: as token count grows, the model's ability to accurately recall information from the middle of context decreases. Place key summaries at the start, use section headers, and ground claims in <quotes> blocks.

q: A subagent returns a 40-field web search result on every call. What is the recommended pattern to keep the orchestrator's context lean?
opts: Forward the full result so the orchestrator can decide what matters
Trim verbose outputs to the 4 relevant fields and forward only those
Always run a separate Claude call to compress the result first
Disable tool result returns entirely and re-issue searches each turn
explain: Effective context engineering recommends trimming verbose tool outputs to only the relevant fields (e.g., 40+ to ~4) before they accumulate in the orchestrator's context. This keeps the active window focused on signal, not noise.

q: Across a 30-turn ticket the agent forgets the customer's order ID and SLA tier. What is the correct remediation?
opts: Increase max_tokens so the model has more room to remember
Maintain a persistent 'case facts' block at the top of the user message each turn
Switch to a smaller model that ignores history
Disable prompt caching so context is fresh each turn
explain: A persistent 'case facts' block summarizing key entities (order ID, SLA tier, customer preferences) at the top of context counters lost-in-the-middle and prevents drift across many turns. Increasing max_tokens does not help with input recall.

q: Which of the following is a valid escalation trigger for human handoff per Anthropic's guidance?
opts: The agent reports low self-confidence on its own answer
An explicit human request, a policy gap, or the agent cannot make progress
The conversation has lasted more than 10 turns
explain: Documented escalation triggers are: explicit human request, a policy gap (no rule covers the situation), or inability to progress. Sentiment and self-confidence are not reliable routing signals.

q: Why is the model's self-reported confidence not a reliable signal for routing decisions?
opts: Self-confidence scores are not exposed in the API
Self-confidence is poorly calibrated and does not consistently track actual correctness
Self-confidence only works on Opus models
Self-confidence resets on every tool call
explain: Model self-confidence is not a calibrated probability of correctness; routing on it produces inconsistent behavior. Use deterministic policy gates (explicit user request, policy gap, no-progress detection) instead.

q: A subagent's web fetch fails with a 503. What should the structured failure context returned to the orchestrator include?
opts: Failure type, attempted action, partial results gathered, and alternative paths
A retry count and nothing else
explain: Structured error propagation should pass: failure type, attempted action, partial results, and viable alternatives. This lets the orchestrator decide between retry, alternate tool, or escalation without losing prior work.

q: A search tool returns zero rows. How should the agent distinguish a genuine empty result from an access failure?
opts: Treat all empty responses as access failures and retry
Inspect status codes and error fields; absent errors plus empty data is a valid empty result
Always escalate empty results to a human
Compare against a cached non-empty result and pick whichever is larger
explain: Distinguish access failures from valid empty results by checking explicit error/status fields. Conflating them either masks outages or fabricates data. Treat 'no error + empty rows' as a real empty answer.

q: Two sources report conflicting GDP statistics for the same country. What is the recommended handling?
opts: Annotate both values with attribution and publication date; do not pick or average
Drop the conflict from the output
explain: For conflicting source statistics, preserve provenance: annotate each claim with source attribution and dates rather than picking or averaging. The downstream consumer needs the disagreement made visible.

q: Claude is investigating a topic across 12 sub-agent runs. Where should it persist findings so they survive context boundaries?
opts: In a scratchpad file written to memory or shared filesystem
In the assistant's prior turn text only
explain: Anthropic's context engineering pillars include structured note-taking via scratchpad files. Persisting findings to memory or shared filesystem state lets work survive context resets, sub-agent boundaries, and compaction.

q: You enable the memory tool. What is the correct tool type identifier and beta header status as of GA?
explain: The memory tool is GA. Use type: 'memory_20250818' with no beta header. The tool is client-side: Claude emits tool_use calls and your application executes file operations under /memories.

q: Where does memory tool execution happen, and what does that imply for storage?
opts: Server-side at Anthropic; storage is managed for you
Client-side in your application; you control where files persist
Inside Claude Code only; storage is at ~/.claude/memory
explain: Memory tool operations are client-side. Claude makes tool calls (view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename); your application executes them locally and decides backing storage (filesystem, DB, encrypted blob, etc.).

q: Which of the following are the six commands the memory tool defines?
opts: read, write, append, search, delete, move
view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename
get, put, patch, delete, list, head
open, close, lock, unlock, save, load
explain: Memory tool commands: view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename. These mirror the text editor tool patterns. Your client must implement handlers for each.

q: A path traversal attempt arrives via a memory tool input: '/memories/../etc/passwd'. What MUST your handler do?
opts: Forward the path to Claude for review
Resolve to canonical form, verify it stays inside /memories, and reject otherwise
Allow the read since memory tool runs server-side
Log the attempt but allow the path
explain: The docs warn that handlers MUST validate all paths to prevent traversal. Reject sequences like ../, ..\, URL-encoded variants (%2e%2e%2f), resolve to canonical form, and confirm the result is under /memories.

q: A coding agent must restrict every memory tool path. Which directory is the documented anchor?
explain: All memory tool operations should be restricted to the /memories directory. Reject any absolute path that doesn't start with /memories after canonicalization.

q: You want compaction enabled for a long-running research orchestrator. What header and strategy do you send?
opts: Header 'compact-2026-01-12' with strategy 'compact_20260112' on supported models
No header; compaction is automatic on every model
explain: Server-side compaction is in beta: include 'compact-2026-01-12' as the beta header and add a 'compact_20260112' edit to context_management.edits. Supported models: Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, Sonnet 4.6.

q: Which model is NOT supported by server-side compaction?
explain: Compaction supports Mythos Preview, Opus 4.7, Opus 4.6, and Sonnet 4.6 only. Haiku 4.5 is not in the supported list.

q: What is the minimum allowed value for the compaction trigger threshold?
explain: Compaction trigger must be at least 50,000 input tokens; the default is 150,000.

q: How does the API continue a conversation after a compaction block is generated?
opts: You must manually delete prior messages on the next request
On subsequent requests, append the response and the API automatically drops blocks prior to the compaction block
The API requires you to base64 encode the summary
explain: After compaction, append the assistant response (which contains the compaction block) to your messages. On the next request, the API automatically drops everything prior to the compaction block and continues from the summary.

q: You need fine-grained tool result clearing instead of full compaction. Which beta header and strategy combination applies?
opts: No header required; clear_tool_uses is GA
explain: Context editing uses the beta header 'context-management-2025-06-27'. Strategies are 'clear_tool_uses_20250919' (tool result clearing) and 'clear_thinking_20251015' (thinking block clearing).

q: Which Claude models support context editing strategies?
explain: Context editing is available on all supported Claude models, unlike compaction which is restricted to Opus 4.7/4.6, Sonnet 4.6, and Mythos.

q: After clear_tool_uses fires, what happens to the prompt cache prefix that included the cleared tool results?
opts: The cache is preserved; clearing is transparent to caching
The cache is invalidated at the point where clearing occurs
Caching is automatically disabled for the session
The cache TTL doubles to compensate
explain: Tool result clearing invalidates cached prefixes when content is removed. Use 'clear_at_least' to ensure each clear removes enough tokens to make the cache rewrite worthwhile.

q: What is the maximum number of cache_control breakpoints allowed per Messages API request?
explain: A maximum of 4 cache_control breakpoints is allowed per request. Plan placement carefully across tools, system, and messages.

q: What is the strict caching hierarchy used by prompt caching?
explain: Caching is ordered tools -> system -> messages. Changes at any earlier level invalidate that level and all subsequent levels.

q: You place cache_control on a per-request user message that varies every call. Why is the cache hit rate near zero?
opts: The cache is global per organization and your workspace lacks permissions
Place breakpoints on the LAST stable block, not on volatile per-request user content
The 5m TTL is too short for caching
You must use 1h TTL for any caching to work
explain: Cache breakpoints must sit on the last stable block. Marking a volatile per-request block changes the prefix hash every request, so nothing matches and writes are wasted.

q: Which TTL options are supported for cache_control?
explain: cache_control supports two TTLs: '5m' (default) and '1h'. Longer TTLs trade higher write cost for longer reuse windows.

q: You mix a 1h cache breakpoint and a 5m cache breakpoint in one request. What ordering rule applies?
opts: The 5m breakpoint must appear before the 1h breakpoint
The 1h breakpoint must appear before the 5m breakpoint
Both must use the same TTL
explain: When mixing TTLs, longer-TTL breakpoints (1h) must appear before shorter-TTL (5m) breakpoints in the request, otherwise validation fails.

q: What is the cache read pricing multiplier vs base input pricing?
explain: Cache reads are 0.1x base input pricing. 5m writes are 1.25x and 1h writes are 2x base.

q: What is the cost multiplier for a 1h cache write vs base input pricing?
explain: 1h cache writes cost 2x base input. 5m writes cost 1.25x base. Reads (any TTL) cost 0.1x base.

q: What is the minimum cacheable token count for Claude Opus 4.7?
explain: Opus 4.7 (and Opus 4.6, 4.5) require a minimum of 4096 tokens before a breakpoint to actually cache. Below that, the request is processed without caching.

q: What is the minimum cacheable token count for Claude Sonnet 4.6?
explain: Sonnet 4.6 minimum is 2048 tokens. Older Sonnet 4.5/4/3.7 used 1024. Opus 4.x and Haiku 4.5 use 4096.

q: What is the minimum cacheable token count for Claude Haiku 4.5?
explain: Haiku 4.5 requires 4096 tokens minimum. Haiku 3.5 used 2048. Sonnet 4.6 uses 2048; Opus 4.x uses 4096.

q: You marked a breakpoint but caching seems inactive. How do you confirm whether caching actually wrote?
opts: The API returns an explicit 'cache_disabled' error
Inspect 'cache_creation_input_tokens' and 'cache_read_input_tokens' in the usage object; if both are 0 the request fell below the minimum
Caching is always active when cache_control is set
Run the same request 3 times and infer from latency
explain: Below-minimum requests silently no-op (no error). Verify caching by checking 'cache_creation_input_tokens' and 'cache_read_input_tokens' in the usage block. Both being 0 means caching did not engage.

q: With auto prompt caching (Feb 2026), where does a top-level cache_control breakpoint get placed?
opts: At the very first content block
Auto-moved to the last cacheable block in the request
explain: Auto prompt caching introduced in Feb 2026 moves a top-level cache_control to the last cacheable block automatically. This avoids the common pitfall of caching a volatile block.

q: How does workspace-level cache isolation (Feb 2026) differ from earlier behavior?
opts: Caches are isolated per workspace; different workspaces cannot share cache hits
Caches are isolated per API key
Caches now require explicit workspace IDs in headers
explain: As of Feb 2026, automatic caching is workspace-scoped on Claude API and Azure AI Foundry preview. Bedrock and Vertex retain organization-level isolation.

q: What does stop_reason: end_turn signal?
opts: The model finished its response naturally
A tool call must be executed
explain: end_turn is the most common stop reason and indicates the model finished its response naturally with no further action needed.

q: Your code receives stop_reason: tool_use. What is the correct next action?
opts: Treat it as an error and retry
Execute the requested tool calls and return results in the next user turn
explain: tool_use means Claude requested tool execution. Run the tool(s), return tool_result blocks in the next user message, and call the API again.

q: You receive stop_reason: max_tokens with a final block of type tool_use. What does the docs recommend?
opts: Retry the same request with a higher max_tokens to obtain the full tool_use block
Ignore the truncation and execute the partial tool input
Switch to a model with a larger context window
explain: If the response is cut off mid tool_use, the partial input may be invalid. Retry with a higher max_tokens so Claude can finish emitting the complete tool_use block.

q: A web_search-enabled request returns stop_reason: pause_turn. What is happening and how do you handle it?
opts: The user paused the request; abort
The server-side sampling loop hit its 10-iteration cap; resend the assistant response back unchanged to continue
An API error; retry with exponential backoff
explain: pause_turn means the server-side server-tool sampling loop reached its iteration cap (default 10). Send the assistant content back as-is in a continuation request to let Claude finish.

q: What does stop_reason: refusal indicate?
opts: Claude declined for safety reasons; consider rephrasing or routing differently
explain: refusal means Claude declined to generate a response due to safety considerations. Document handling involves rephrasing, switching to a model with different filters (e.g., Haiku 4.5 in some cases), or escalating.

q: What does stop_reason: model_context_window_exceeded mean and which models return it by default?
opts: The full request hit the context window before max_tokens did; default in Sonnet 4.5+ and newer models
An API error returned with HTTP 429
explain: model_context_window_exceeded indicates the response hit the context window cap before max_tokens. Default in Sonnet 4.5 and newer; older models need beta header 'model-context-window-exceeded-2025-08-26'.

q: How many iterations does the server-tool sampling loop run before returning pause_turn by default?
explain: The server-side sampling loop for server tools (web_search, web_fetch) defaults to 10 iterations. On hitting the cap, the API returns pause_turn so you can choose to continue.

q: In the Claude Agent SDK (Python), how do two query() calls share context within one process without manual ID tracking?
opts: Use ClaudeSDKClient as an async context manager so each client.query() continues the same session automatically
explain: ClaudeSDKClient holds the session ID internally. Each client.query() automatically continues the same session within an async context manager.

q: In the TypeScript Agent SDK V1 query() function, how do you resume the most recent session in the current directory?
opts: Pass continue: true on the next query() call
explain: The stable TS SDK uses continue: true to resume the most recent session in the current cwd. No ID tracking required. resume: <id> targets a specific session.

q: You captured a session_id and want to branch to try a different approach without losing the original. What option do you set?
opts: fork_session: True (Python) / forkSession: true (TS) along with resume
explain: forkSession copies the existing session's history into a new session with a distinct ID; the original is unchanged. You set fork_session=True (Python) or forkSession: true (TS) together with resume.

q: Where are Agent SDK session JSONL transcripts stored on disk?
explain: Sessions are stored at ~/.claude/projects/<encoded-cwd>/<session-id>.jsonl where <encoded-cwd> replaces non-alphanumeric chars with '-'. resume must run from the matching cwd or the SDK looks in the wrong place.
